text.title: Stakeholder Management: Identification and Mapping Framework
text.summary: Stakeholder types, Power/Interest grid with mandatory attitude dimension, engagement levels, and the four-step identification-to-execution process.

list.item: End Users: customers, employees — need value from product
Decision Makers: executives, sponsors — need business outcomes
Influencers: advisors, thought leaders — need credibility and early access
Builders: developers, designers — need clear requirements
Support: customer success, sales — need product knowledge
External: partners, regulators — need compliance and compatibility

rule.statement: Always add an explicit attitude column (Supporter / Neutral / Resistor) to the Power/Interest grid. Mark Unknown when attitude cannot be inferred from a cited source. Never leave attitude blank.
rule.rationale: A Power/Interest grid without the attitude dimension is a 2D map of a 3D problem. A high-power stakeholder coded as "Manage Closely" but secretly a Resistor is the silent killer of cross-functional product launches.

list.item: High Power, High Interest → Manage Closely: engage deeply, regular bidirectional updates, co-creation where possible
High Power, Low Interest → Keep Satisfied: periodic updates, frame in terms of business outcomes, not details
Low Power, High Interest → Keep Informed: involve in feedback sessions, one-way updates are sufficient for most
Low Power, Low Interest → Monitor: minimal effort, periodic check-ins only
Partner: active collaboration, co-creation, regular meetings, bidirectional input loops
Involved: regular input, reviews, feedback sessions

rule.statement: Every stakeholder must have a named engagement cadence (weekly 1:1, biweekly email, monthly review). "Ad-hoc" is code for "nothing happens".
rule.rationale: A communication plan without a concrete cadence produces the same outcome as no plan — stakeholders feel ignored, resistors go undetected, and late-stage blockers emerge at critical milestones.

list.item: Step 1 — Identify: who uses, pays, approves, builds, supports, or could block this product
Step 2 — Analyze: what they want, what they fear, power level, interest level, attitude
Step 3 — Plan engagement: cadence, format, information they need, relationship owner
Step 4 — Execute and adapt: regular touchpoints, track attitude shifts, handle conflicts proactively
